description: If A implies B, then the falsity of B implies the falsity of A.

hints.text: This is the **contrapositive**: if $A$ [implication|implies] $B$, then $\lnot B$ implies $\lnot A$. (If $B$ is impossible, then $A$ must not hold either — otherwise $A$ would force $B$.) You'll need `not-intro` to prove $\lnot A$. Start by assuming $A \to B$.
Now you're proving $\lnot B \to \lnot A$. As always with [implication|implications], assume the antecedent: $\lnot B$.
To prove $\lnot A$ using `not-intro`, you need two subproofs sharing $A$ — one proving some $p$, the other proving $\lnot p$. For $p$, choose $B$. First subproof: assume $A$.
From $A \to B$ (step 1) and your fresh $A$ (step 3), use [modus-ponens|modus ponens] to get $B$. This is your positive subproof — [context|context] contains $A$, [conclusion|conclusion] is $B$.

Second subproof: you need a step whose [context|context] contains $A$ and whose [conclusion|conclusion] is $\lnot B$. Use `extend` to add $A$ to step 2's context — this is **weakening**: the conclusion $\lnot B$ still follows with the extra assumption.
Steps 4 and 6 both have $A$ in [context|context] and prove $B$ and $\lnot B$ — the [contradiction|contradiction]. Apply `not-intro A`.
